title: 💵 Roth IRA 2026: New Limits, Phase-Outs and the Backdoor Loophole
description:
Complete 2026 Roth IRA guide with new IRS contribution limits, MAGI phase-out brackets, catch-up rules, and the backdoor Roth strategy still legal under SECURE Act 2.0.

Sources: IRS Notice 2025-83 Inflation Adjustments, IRS Publication 590-A, SECURE Act 2.0 provisions.

segments.voice: Everything you have been told about the Roth IRA contribution limits is now outdated. IRS Notice 2025-83 just raised the limits for 2026, changed the income phase out brackets, and adjusted the catch up provisions for older savers. If you contribute the old amounts, you leave money on the table. If you ignore the new phase out brackets, you trigger excess contribution penalties of 6 percent per year on the overage until you fix it. This 8 minute video saves you both problems.
segments.visual: IRS notice 2025-83 document Roth IRA contribution limits 2026 raised

segments.voice: The Roth IRA contribution limit for 2026 is now 7,500 dollars for individuals under age 50. That is a 500 dollar increase from the 2025 limit of 7,000 dollars. The catch up contribution for those age 50 and older adds 1,100 dollars, bringing the total to 8,600 dollars for older savers. The catch up amount itself was adjusted under SECURE Act 2.0 inflation indexing rules that took effect in 2025.
segments.visual: IRS contribution limit chart 7500 8600 dollars 2026 Roth IRA increase

segments.voice: Roth IRA contributions are made with after tax dollars. You pay income tax now, the money grows tax free for decades, and qualified withdrawals in retirement are completely tax free. There is no required minimum distribution during your lifetime as the original owner. This makes the Roth IRA the most valuable retirement vehicle in the US tax code for most savers. JL Collins, author of The Simple Path to Wealth, calls the Roth IRA the closest thing to a free lunch in personal finance.
segments.visual: Tax free retirement growth chart compounding decades after tax contributions

segments.voice: The income phase out is where most people get tripped up. To contribute to a Roth IRA, your modified adjusted gross income must be below certain thresholds. For single filers in 2026, full contributions are allowed up to 161,000 dollars MAGI, with the phase out range extending to 176,000 dollars. Above 176,000 you cannot directly contribute to a Roth IRA at all. For married filing jointly, full contributions are allowed up to 246,000 MAGI, phasing out at 266,000.
segments.visual: Phase out brackets income limits single married filing jointly 2026 chart

segments.voice: MAGI calculation trips up high earners. Modified adjusted gross income starts with your AGI from line 11 of Form 1040, then adds back certain deductions including student loan interest, tuition and fees deduction, foreign earned income exclusion, and traditional IRA deduction. For most W-2 earners, MAGI is very close to AGI. For self employed with foreign income or international workers, the adjustment matters. Use IRS Publication 590-A Worksheet 2-1 to calculate it precisely.
segments.visual: Form 1040 MAGI calculation worksheet adjusted gross income IRS publication 590

segments.voice: The partial contribution math in the phase out range. If your MAGI falls between the lower and upper limits of the phase out, you can make a partial contribution. The formula is your full limit times the fraction of how far you are into the phase out range. A single filer with 170,000 MAGI is 60 percent through the phase out range, so their allowed contribution is 7,500 times 40 percent equals 3,000 dollars. Always round down to the nearest 10 dollars per IRS rules.
segments.visual: Phase out calculation example partial contribution allowed 3000 dollars math

segments.voice: The backdoor Roth IRA strategy is still legal in 2026. If your income exceeds the direct Roth contribution limits, you can contribute to a non deductible traditional IRA and immediately convert to a Roth IRA. There is no income limit on conversions. This strategy has survived multiple legislative attempts to close it, including the Build Back Better Act that died in 2022. SECURE Act 2.0 did not eliminate it. The IRS has issued no guidance against the strategy when executed correctly.
segments.visual: Backdoor Roth IRA flowchart traditional to Roth conversion strategy diagram

segments.voice: The backdoor Roth execution steps. Step one, contribute up to 7,500 dollars to a traditional IRA. If you have no other deductible traditional IRA balance, the contribution is non deductible. Step two, immediately convert that traditional IRA to a Roth IRA. Use the conversion form at Vanguard, Fidelity, or Schwab. Step three, file Form 8606 with your tax return to track the after tax basis. Step four, repeat next year. Mike Piper at Oblivious Investor has the most accessible breakdown of the mechanics.
segments.visual: Backdoor Roth steps Vanguard Fidelity Schwab conversion form 8606

segments.voice: The pro rata rule is the silent backdoor Roth killer. If you have existing pre tax money in any traditional IRA, SEP IRA, or SIMPLE IRA, the IRS treats the conversion as proportionally taxable based on your total IRA balance. If you have 90,000 in a rollover traditional IRA and add 7,500 non deductible, then convert 7,500, the IRS says 92.3 percent of that conversion is taxable because only 7.7 percent of your total IRA balance was after tax. This wrecks the strategy.
segments.visual: Pro rata rule diagram pre tax after tax IRA balance proportional taxation

segments.voice: How to neutralize the pro rata problem. If you have a pre tax rollover IRA, roll it into your current employer 401k before doing the backdoor Roth. The 401k balance does not count toward the pro rata calculation. Only traditional IRAs, SEP IRAs, and SIMPLE IRAs count. Check that your 401k plan accepts incoming rollovers. Most large employer plans at companies like Google, Amazon, and Microsoft do. This single move can unlock the backdoor Roth strategy for high earners.
segments.visual: Rollover IRA to 401k pro rata avoidance strategy clean slate planning

segments.voice: Roth IRA versus traditional IRA. The fundamental question is whether you expect your marginal tax rate in retirement to be higher or lower than your current rate. If higher, choose Roth and pay taxes now. If lower, choose traditional and defer taxes. Conventional wisdom assumes retirement income will be lower, but this is increasingly wrong. With Social Security, pension income, required minimum distributions, and inflation pushing tax brackets, many retirees end up in the same or higher brackets than their working years.
segments.visual: Tax bracket comparison working years versus retirement income tax rates

segments.voice: The case for Roth even at high current tax rates. The Roth IRA has 4 advantages beyond simple tax bracket arbitrage. First, no required minimum distributions during your lifetime. Second, tax free legacy planning for heirs under the SECURE Act 10 year rule. Third, hedge against future tax rate increases when the federal debt eventually forces higher rates. Fourth, tax diversification optionality in retirement to manage your taxable income strategically year by year.
segments.visual: Four advantages Roth no RMD legacy tax rate hedge diversification icons

segments.voice: Spousal Roth IRA is overlooked. A non working spouse can contribute to a Roth IRA based on the working spouse income. The contribution limit is the same 7,500 dollars individually plus 1,100 catch up if 50 or older. The household contribution limit is therefore 15,000 dollars across both spouses if both under 50, or 17,200 if both are over 50. This is one of the most powerful tax shelters available to single income households with a stay at home parent.
segments.visual: Spousal Roth IRA non working spouse stay at home parent contribution

segments.voice: Roth IRA for kids and teenagers. A custodial Roth IRA can be opened for any minor with earned income. Mowing lawns, babysitting, summer camp jobs all qualify if reported properly. The contribution limit is the lower of the 7,500 dollar standard limit or the child total earned income. A 16 year old earning 4,000 dollars from a summer job can contribute up to 4,000 to a custodial Roth IRA. 50 years of tax free compounding from age 16 to age 66 turns 4,000 into approximately 188,000 dollars at 8 percent annual returns.
segments.visual: Custodial Roth IRA teenager summer job earned income compounding decades

segments.voice: Withdrawal rules for the Roth IRA. Contributions can be withdrawn at any time without taxes or penalties because you already paid the tax on them. This makes the Roth IRA function as a secondary emergency fund for younger savers. Earnings on those contributions cannot be withdrawn without taxes and penalties until age 59 and a half and the account has existed at least 5 years. The 5 year rule applies to each Roth IRA conversion separately, which matters for the Roth conversion ladder strategy.
segments.visual: Roth IRA withdrawal rules contributions earnings 59 and a half 5 year rule

segments.voice: The qualified withdrawals are completely tax free in retirement. Once you hit 59 and a half and the account is at least 5 years old, every dollar of growth comes out tax free. A Roth IRA contributed at 7,500 per year for 35 years with average 8 percent returns grows to approximately 1.4 million dollars. None of that is taxed. Compare to a taxable brokerage account where capital gains and dividends create annual tax drag and final liquidation creates additional capital gains taxes.
segments.visual: Roth IRA 35 years compounding 1.4 million dollars tax free retirement

segments.voice: Special Roth IRA exceptions to the early withdrawal penalty. The 10 percent penalty on early withdrawal of earnings has exceptions for first time home purchase up to 10,000 dollars lifetime, qualified higher education expenses, birth or adoption expenses up to 5,000 dollars, disability, and substantially equal periodic payments. The earnings withdrawal still owes ordinary income tax in some cases, but the 10 percent penalty is waived. IRS Publication 590-B has the complete list of exceptions.
segments.visual: Roth IRA early withdrawal exceptions home purchase education first time buyer

segments.voice: The investment selection inside the Roth IRA. The Roth IRA is the tax shelter. You still need to pick investments. The classic recommendation from Vanguard founder John Bogle was a 3 fund portfolio. Total US Stock Market, total international stock, and total bond market. With 7,500 dollars per year and a long time horizon, many advisors recommend 100 percent US stock index funds for accounts under 250,000 dollars. The Schwab S&P 500 Index Fund SWPPX and the Fidelity Total Market Index FZROX both have zero expense ratios.
segments.visual: Three fund portfolio Vanguard Bogle US stocks international bonds allocation

segments.voice: The asset location strategy. Because Roth IRAs generate tax free growth, the conventional wisdom is to hold the highest expected return assets inside the Roth and the lower return income assets like bonds in tax advantaged or taxable accounts. A high income earner might hold 100 percent emerging markets stocks in the Roth IRA, US large cap in the 401k, and bonds in the taxable account. This optimizes after tax returns over decades. The math is complex but the principle is simple.
segments.visual: Asset location strategy diagram Roth high growth 401k mid taxable bonds

segments.voice: Common mistake number one. Forgetting to invest the contribution. You transfer 7,500 dollars from your bank to your Vanguard Roth IRA. It sits in the settlement money market fund. You think you bought VTSAX. You did not. The money sits earning 4.95 percent instead of compounding in stocks for 30 years. Always verify the trade executed after every contribution. This single mistake costs people hundreds of thousands of dollars over a career.
segments.visual: Roth IRA contribution uninvested cash mistake settlement fund money market

segments.voice: Common mistake number two. Contributing while above the income phase out limit. The IRS imposes a 6 percent excess contribution penalty every year the excess remains in the account. If you contributed 7,500 dollars but the phase out math allowed only 3,000, the 4,500 excess generates a 270 dollar penalty every year until withdrawn. Always calculate MAGI before contributing or use the backdoor Roth path if uncertain about income for the year.
segments.visual: IRS excess contribution penalty 6 percent annual fee mistake calculation

segments.voice: Common mistake number three. Forgetting to file Form 8606 with the backdoor Roth. Form 8606 reports your basis in the non deductible traditional IRA contribution. Without this form, the IRS does not know the contribution was after tax and may tax the conversion. The penalty for not filing 8606 is 50 dollars per missed form. Most importantly, your tax records become wrong and decades later your heirs may pay tax twice on the same money.
segments.visual: Form 8606 IRS reporting after tax basis non deductible IRA contribution

segments.voice: Common mistake number four. Roth IRA contribution timing. The contribution deadline is April 15 of the following year. Many people make their 2026 Roth IRA contribution in April 2027. That is allowed but wastes 16 months of tax free compounding. Funding the Roth IRA in January of the contribution year captures the maximum compounding period. On 35 years of contributions, this timing alone adds approximately 60,000 dollars to the final balance at 8 percent returns.
segments.visual: January 1 versus April 15 Roth IRA funding timing compounding advantage

segments.voice: The mega backdoor Roth IRA strategy. If your employer 401k plan allows after tax contributions and in service distributions, you can contribute up to 70,000 dollars total in 2026 to your 401k including employer match, traditional, and after tax contributions. The after tax portion can be rolled to a Roth IRA. This converts an extra 23,000 to 30,000 dollars per year into Roth space. Only about 22 percent of 401k plans allow this according to the Plan Sponsor Council of America 2024 survey. Check your summary plan description.
segments.visual: Mega backdoor Roth 401k after tax contribution in service rollover diagram

segments.voice: But here is what nobody talks about. The Roth IRA is also the best estate planning tool for middle class families. Under SECURE Act 2.0, non spouse beneficiaries must drain inherited Roth IRAs within 10 years. But all distributions remain tax free. Your kids inherit a fully tax free account they can let grow for 10 more years before drawing. Compare to a 401k or traditional IRA inheritance where all distributions are taxed at the heir ordinary income rate, often when they are in their peak earning years.
segments.visual: Roth IRA inheritance estate planning children tax free SECURE Act 10 year rule

segments.voice: Your action plan. Step one, verify your 2026 MAGI will be below the phase out range. Step two, if eligible, contribute 7,500 dollars directly to a Roth IRA at Vanguard, Fidelity, or Schwab in January. Step three, if above phase out, execute the backdoor Roth strategy and file Form 8606. Step four, invest the contribution immediately in index funds. Step five, verify the trade executed. Next video, the dark side of traditional 401k accounts. The retirement tax bomb that hits at age 73 and what to do about it. The numbers will change how you think about pre tax contributions forever.
segments.visual: Action plan checklist Roth IRA 2026 contribution backdoor invest verify
